title: TokenStream::Empty state
description: Represents an empty token stream encoded as None in the inner Option; acts as the identity for concatenation.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- Inner representation is None (no underlying bridge::client::TokenStream)
- Appending a single non-empty stream can replace it directly without concatenation
- Empty streams are ignored when collected into ConcatStreamsHelper

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Empty via ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to when helper.streams is empty
- Empty -> NonEmpty via ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to when helper.streams.len() == 1 (direct move)
- Empty -> NonEmpty via ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to when helper.streams.len() > 1 (concatenation)

**Evidence:** line 16: if let Some(stream) = stream.0 — empty TokenStream (None) is skipped; line 23: TokenStream(self.streams.pop()) — when helper is empty, this yields TokenStream(None); line 33: let base = stream.0.take(); — base can be None (empty); line 34: if base.is_none() && self.streams.len() == 1 { ... } — optimized path for Empty base with exactly one appended stream

**Implementation:** Introduce NonEmptyTokenStream newtype (or TokenStream<NonEmpty>) guaranteeing inner Some(..). Provide TryFrom<TokenStream> for NonEmptyTokenStream. Update helpers to accept NonEmptyTokenStream so empty cases are eliminated at compile time; concatenation APIs then do not need to branch on None.

title: TokenStream::NonEmpty state
description: Represents a token stream with content encoded as Some(inner) in the Option; concatenation always produces a Some(..).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** NonEmpty

**State invariants:**
- Inner representation is Some(bridge::client::TokenStream)
- When appended to, concatenation executes regardless of helper size (unless helper is empty, which is a no-op)
- Collection into ConcatStreamsHelper always pushes the inner stream

**Transitions:**
- NonEmpty -> NonEmpty via ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to with any non-empty helper (concatenation)
- NonEmpty -> NonEmpty via ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to with empty helper (no-op)
- — Produced by ConcatStreamsHelper::build when helper.streams.len() == 1 (direct move) or > 1 (concatenation)

**Evidence:** line 16: if let Some(stream) = stream.0 — NonEmpty case yields Some(..) and is pushed; line 25: TokenStream(Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(None, self.streams))) — build produces a guaranteed Some(..) for multiple inputs; line 37: stream.0 = Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(base, self.streams)); — appending results in Some(..) regardless of base when helper has >= 1 stream

**Implementation:** Model TokenStream<S> with S ∈ {Empty, NonEmpty}. Provide concatenation only for NonEmpty or for (Empty, NonEmpty) pairs returning NonEmpty. Builders return TokenStream<Empty> or TokenStream<NonEmpty> explicitly, removing the need for Option checks.

title: ConcatStreamsHelper::Empty buffer
description: No accumulated streams; appending does nothing and building yields an empty TokenStream.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatStreamsHelper

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- streams.is_empty() == true
- append_to is a no-op
- build returns TokenStream(None)

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Singleton via push(non-empty TokenStream)
- Empty -> Empty via push(empty TokenStream)
- Consumed via build(self) or append_to(self, _)

**Evidence:** line 30: if self.streams.is_empty() { return; } — append_to is a no-op when empty; line 16: if let Some(stream) = stream.0 { ... } — push ignores empty TokenStream; line 23: TokenStream(self.streams.pop()) — with len == 0, pop() is None, yielding an empty TokenStream

**Implementation:** Use a typestate builder ConcatStreamsHelper<S> where S encodes Empty vs NonEmpty. new() -> ConcatStreamsHelper<Empty>; push(NonEmptyTokenStream) -> ConcatStreamsHelper<NonEmpty>. This removes empty-case branches in append_to/build.

title: ConcatStreamsHelper::Singleton buffer
description: Exactly one accumulated stream; building or appending can move it directly without concatenation, depending on base.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatStreamsHelper

**State:** Singleton

**State invariants:**
- streams.len() == 1
- build returns the single inner stream directly
- If base is empty, append_to moves the single stream without concatenation

**Transitions:**
- Singleton -> Multiple via push(non-empty TokenStream)
- Singleton -> Singleton via push(empty TokenStream)
- Consumed via build(self) or append_to(self, _)

**Evidence:** line 22: if self.streams.len() <= 1 { ... } — singleton handled without concatenation; line 23: TokenStream(self.streams.pop()) — direct move of the single stream; line 34: if base.is_none() && self.streams.len() == 1 { stream.0 = self.streams.pop(); } — optimized append without concat

**Implementation:** Refine the builder states to track Singleton explicitly (e.g., ConcatStreamsHelper<Singleton>) enabling specialized operations without runtime len checks.

title: ConcatStreamsHelper::Multiple buffer
description: Two or more accumulated streams; requires concatenation to build or append.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatStreamsHelper

**State:** Multiple

**State invariants:**
- streams.len() > 1
- build must call BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(None, streams)
- append_to must call BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(base, streams)

**Transitions:**
- Consumed via build(self) or append_to(self, _)

**Evidence:** line 24-26: else { TokenStream(Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(None, self.streams))) } — concatenation for multiple inputs; line 36-37: else { stream.0 = Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_streams(base, self.streams)); } — concatenation path in append_to

**Implementation:** Track the NonEmpty aggregate state and use a NonEmptyTokenStream vector type guaranteeing len >= 1; for len > 1, a dedicated Multiple state can avoid branching. APIs would statically encode when concatenation is required.

title: ConcatTreesHelper::Empty state
description: Helper has collected no TokenTree items; build/append avoid invoking concatenation and either produce an empty TokenStream or no-op.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatTreesHelper

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- trees.is_empty() == true
- build(self) constructs TokenStream(None) (an empty stream)
- append_to(self, stream) returns early without modifying stream

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> NonEmpty via push(tree)
- Empty -> Consumed via build(self)
- Empty -> Consumed via append_to(self, &mut TokenStream)

**Evidence:** line 6: struct ConcatTreesHelper { trees: Vec<...> } — emptiness tracked at runtime via Vec length; line 26: if self.trees.is_empty() — branch on runtime emptiness; line 27: TokenStream(None) — build returns empty stream when helper is empty; line 34: if self.trees.is_empty() { return; } — early return in append_to when empty

**Implementation:** Model as ConcatTreesHelper<S> with S ∈ {Empty, NonEmpty}. new() -> ConcatTreesHelper<Empty>; push(self, tree) -> ConcatTreesHelper<NonEmpty>; push on NonEmpty returns Self; provide build()/append_to() only for ConcatTreesHelper<NonEmpty> (and optionally a trivial build_empty() for Empty) to remove runtime emptiness checks.

title: ConcatTreesHelper::NonEmpty state
description: Helper has at least one TokenTree; building or appending will invoke BridgeMethods::ts_concat_trees and produce a non-empty TokenStream.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** ConcatTreesHelper

**State:** NonEmpty

**State invariants:**
- trees.is_empty() == false
- build(self) constructs TokenStream(Some(...))
- append_to(self, stream) calls ts_concat_trees(stream.0.take(), self.trees) and assigns Some(...) back

**Transitions:**
- NonEmpty -> Consumed via build(self)
- NonEmpty -> Consumed via append_to(self, &mut TokenStream)

**Evidence:** line 29: TokenStream(Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_trees(None, self.trees))) — non-empty helper builds a non-empty stream; line 37: stream.0 = Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_trees(stream.0.take(), self.trees)) — appending uses and produces Some(...), i.e., non-empty

**Implementation:** Expose build()/append_to() only on ConcatTreesHelper<NonEmpty>; keep push() available to transition from Empty to NonEmpty. This removes the need for is_empty() branching and enforces correct call sites at compile time.

title: TokenStream::Empty state
description: A TokenStream that contains no tokens, represented by None internally.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- Internally represented as TokenStream(None)
- Concatenation with a non-empty helper transitions to a non-empty state

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> NonEmpty via ConcatTreesHelper::build with a NonEmpty helper (uses ts_concat_trees(None, trees))
- Empty -> NonEmpty via ConcatTreesHelper::append_to with a NonEmpty helper (assigns Some(...))

**Evidence:** line 27: TokenStream(None) — explicit empty representation; line 29: ts_concat_trees(None, self.trees) wrapped in Some(...) — building from an empty base yields non-empty; line 37: stream.0 = Some(...) — appending non-empty helper to possibly-empty stream produces non-empty

**Implementation:** Introduce a NonEmptyTokenStream newtype (or TokenStream<NonEmpty>) to represent a guaranteed non-empty stream. Provide conversions/builders that only yield NonEmptyTokenStream when there are tokens, and keep TokenStream (or TokenStream<Empty>) for the empty case. Concatenation APIs can then avoid Option and accept a NonEmptyTokenStream for the accumulator.

title: TokenStream::NonEmpty state
description: A TokenStream with at least one token, represented by Some(inner) internally; further concatenations remain non-empty.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** NonEmpty

**State invariants:**
- Internally represented as TokenStream(Some(...))
- append_to with a non-empty helper keeps the stream non-empty

**Transitions:**
- NonEmpty -> NonEmpty via ConcatTreesHelper::append_to with a NonEmpty helper

**Evidence:** line 29: TokenStream(Some(...)) — explicit non-empty representation on build from a non-empty helper; line 37: stream.0 = Some(BridgeMethods::ts_concat_trees(stream.0.take(), self.trees)) — appending assigns Some(...) ensuring non-empty

**Implementation:** Use a NonEmptyTokenStream type (or TokenStream<NonEmpty>) so APIs that require content cannot be called on an empty stream. Methods like append can take and return NonEmptyTokenStream, removing runtime Some/None handling.

title: __rust_start_panic::Aborting behavior
description: Calling __rust_start_panic attaches a platform-specific abort message (if supported) and then unconditionally aborts the process; control flow never returns to the caller.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_start_panic

**State:** Aborting

**State invariants:**
- Function does not return; process termination is guaranteed by calling a divergent function
- Payload is intentionally not dropped ("leaked") so a message can be attached on supported platforms
- No unwinding occurs on this panic path

**Evidence:** line 31: comment states "Leak" the payload and shim to the relevant abort; lines 35-38: android::android_set_abort_message(_payload) uses the payload for abort message; lines 39-42: zkvm::zkvm_set_abort_message(_payload) uses the payload for abort message; lines 44-47: declares extern safe fn __rust_abort() -> ! (diverges); line 50: calls __rust_abort(), ensuring no return

**Implementation:** Encode the non-returning behavior in the type system by making __rust_start_panic return the never type (!) so call sites cannot assume it returns. More broadly, expose the panic runtime as a type-level state (e.g., PanicRuntime<Abort>) where APIs reachable only in the Abort state have divergent signatures.

title: __rust_panic_cleanup::Unreachable
description: This cleanup entry point must never be invoked in the panic=abort runtime; it immediately panics with unreachable! if called.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_panic_cleanup

**State:** Unreachable

**State invariants:**
- Function is not a valid code path under panic=abort
- Any call indicates a violated precondition

**Evidence:** lines 27-29: function body is just unreachable!(), asserting it should never be called

**Implementation:** Statically prevent this path by removing it from callable surfaces under panic=abort or by changing its signature to return ! to encode that no valid return is possible. Alternatively, gate any potential callers behind a type-level marker indicating unwinding support is disabled.

title: rust_eh_catch_typeinfo::Unused placeholder
description: A linker placeholder for Emscripten catch typeinfo that is never used with -C panic=abort.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** personalities::rust_eh_catch_typeinfo

**State:** Unused

**State invariants:**
- Panics do not generate exceptions with panic=abort
- Foreign exceptions are currently UB with panic=abort
- catch_unwind will never consult this typeinfo

**Evidence:** lines 84-89: comments state panics don't generate exceptions and catch_unwind will never use this typeinfo; lines 90-93: the static is defined but private, reinforcing placeholder status

**Implementation:** Keep the symbol opaque to Rust code (e.g., wrap in a private newtype/module) so it cannot be referenced from safe APIs; this preserves the invariant that it is link-only and never used at runtime.

title: TokenStream::Empty (None-backed) state
description: Represents an empty token stream encoded as None; many operations treat this specially.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == None
- is_empty() returns true
- expand_expr() returns Err(ExpandError)
- into_iter() yields zero items

**Transitions:**
- constructed via new() -> Empty
- Empty -> Backed via From<TokenTree>::from(_) producing a new TokenStream
- Empty -> Backed via FromStr::from_str(_) producing a new TokenStream

**Evidence:** line 6: TokenStream(Option<bridge::client::TokenStream>) — Option encodes presence/absence; line 22-24: new() returns TokenStream(None); line 28-30: is_empty() unwrap_or(true) when self.0 is None; line 43-45: expand_expr uses self.0.as_ref().ok_or(ExpandError)? — None maps to Err; line 133-136: into_iter unwrap_or_default() when self.0 is None, yielding an empty iterator

**Implementation:** Introduce TokenStream<S> with states Empty and Backed. new() -> TokenStream<Empty>. Conversions (From<TokenTree>, FromStr) return TokenStream<Backed>. Provide expand_expr only for TokenStream<Backed>. Option is eliminated; Empty and Backed are tracked at the type level.

title: TokenStream::Backed (Some) state
description: Represents a stream backed by a bridge handle (Some). May still be logically empty, but it has an underlying handle so operations like expand_expr are permitted.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Backed

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == Some(handle)
- expand_expr() is callable and delegates to BridgeMethods::ts_expand_expr
- is_empty() equality determined by BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty(handle)
- into_iter() consumes the handle via BridgeMethods::ts_into_trees

**Transitions:**
- Produced by From<TokenTree>::from(_)
- Produced by FromStr::from_str(_)
- Backed -> Backed via expand_expr() on success (returns a new Backed TokenStream)

**Evidence:** line 6: TokenStream holds Option<bridge::client::TokenStream> — Some encodes backed state; line 71-73: From<TokenTree> constructs TokenStream(Some(...)); line 60-62: FromStr constructs TokenStream(Some(...)) on success; line 43-48: expand_expr requires Some(_) and returns Ok(TokenStream(Some(_))) on success; line 28-30: is_empty() consults BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty(handle) when Some; line 133-136: into_iter maps Some(v) to BridgeMethods::ts_into_trees(v)

**Implementation:** Define TokenStream<Backed> where methods like expand_expr are available; constructors like From<TokenTree> and FromStr return TokenStream<Backed>. Eliminate runtime Option checks by making Empty and Backed distinct types.

title: Panic payload::Box-owned state
description: The panic payload is owned by a Box<dyn Any + Send + 'static>; memory and drop are managed by Rust. It can be safely passed to platform-specific panic handling or converted to a raw pointer for FFI transfer.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_panic_cleanup / __rust_start_panic (panic payload across FFI)

**State:** BoxOwned

**State invariants:**
- Unique ownership via Box<dyn Any + Send + 'static>
- Dropping happens exactly once while in this state
- It must not simultaneously be represented as a raw pointer that will be reboxed elsewhere

**Transitions:**
- BoxOwned -> RawPtr via __rust_panic_cleanup using Box::into_raw(imp::cleanup(..))
- BoxOwned -> Consumed by platform runtime via imp::panic(payload)

**Evidence:** line 98: Box::into_raw(imp::cleanup(payload)) converts a Box to a raw pointer (BoxOwned -> RawPtr); line 108: imp::panic(payload) consumes a Box<..> (uses BoxOwned state); line 97: __rust_panic_cleanup(..) returns *mut (dyn Any + Send + 'static), indicating the Box is converted to a raw pointer for FFI

**Implementation:** Introduce a newtype Payload<Boxed>(Box<dyn Any + Send + 'static>) and a paired Payload<Raw>(*mut (dyn Any + Send + 'static>)). Provide explicit, state-changing constructors: Payload<Boxed>::into_raw(self) -> Payload<Raw> and unsafe Payload<Raw>::from_raw(ptr) -> Payload<Boxed>. Convert immediately at FFI boundaries so the rest of the code never handles bare raw pointers.

title: Panic payload::Raw-pointer state
description: The panic payload is represented as a raw pointer produced from a Box. In this state, the pointer must be reboxed exactly once (or otherwise consumed by the designated runtime) to avoid leaks or double-frees.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** __rust_panic_cleanup / __rust_start_panic (panic payload across FFI)

**State:** RawPtr

**State invariants:**
- Pointer was produced by Box::into_raw or PanicPayload::take_box and points to a valid Box allocation for dyn Any + Send + 'static
- Must be converted back to Box exactly once with Box::from_raw before being dropped
- Pointer must not be aliased for multiple Box::from_raw calls or freed by other means

**Transitions:**
- RawPtr -> BoxOwned via Box::from_raw in __rust_start_panic (payload.take_box() then Box::from_raw(..))
- RawPtr -> BoxOwned by the recipient of __rust_panic_cleanup's return value via Box::from_raw(..) (outside this snippet)

**Evidence:** line 97: __rust_panic_cleanup(payload: *mut u8) -> *mut (dyn Any + Send + 'static) exposes the payload as a raw pointer; line 98: Box::into_raw(..) creates the raw pointer, implying reboxing is required later; line 106: let payload = Box::from_raw(payload.take_box()); reboxes a raw pointer obtained from PanicPayload::take_box() (RawPtr -> BoxOwned)

**Implementation:** Model the payload as Payload<S> with PhantomData state S = Raw or Boxed. Provide only the valid operations per state: impl Payload<Raw> { unsafe fn into_boxed(self) -> Payload<Boxed> } and impl Payload<Boxed> { fn into_raw(self) -> Payload<Raw> }. Keep extern "C" signatures but immediately convert to the appropriate newtype on entry/exit to make misuse inside the crate a compile-time error.

title: Ident::Normal identifier
description: An identifier constructed via Ident::new that is not a raw identifier.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Normal (non-raw)

**State invariants:**
- Underlying bridge::Ident.is_raw == false
- Input string is a valid Rust identifier (including keywords like `self` or `fn`); otherwise construction panics
- Constructed identifier is NFC-normalized

**Evidence:** line 11-12: Docs state the string must be a valid identifier or the function will panic; line 14: Docs state the constructed identifier will be NFC-normalized; line 33-39: Ident::new initializes bridge::Ident; line 35: Calls Symbol::new_ident(string, false) — non-raw mode; line 36: Sets is_raw: false

**Implementation:** Introduce Ident<S> with zero-sized markers Normal and Raw. Provide constructors new(&str, Span) -> Ident<Normal> and new_raw(&str, Span) -> Ident<Raw>. Common methods like span()/set_span() are implemented for any S. This encodes the is_raw boolean at the type level.

title: Ident::Raw identifier
description: An identifier constructed via Ident::new_raw that is a raw identifier (r#ident).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Raw

**State invariants:**
- Underlying bridge::Ident.is_raw == true
- Input string is a valid Rust identifier but not a keyword usable in path segments (e.g., `self`, `super`); otherwise construction panics
- Constructed identifier is NFC-normalized

**Evidence:** line 41-44: Docs state raw identifiers reject path-segment keywords and will panic on such input; line 14: Docs state the constructed identifier will be NFC-normalized; line 46-52: Ident::new_raw initializes bridge::Ident; line 48: Calls Symbol::new_ident(string, true) — raw mode; line 49: Sets is_raw: true

**Implementation:** Model rawness at the type level with Ident<Raw>. new_raw(&str, Span) returns Ident<Raw>. This prevents mixing raw/non-raw where APIs might later diverge, and makes the is_raw state a compile-time fact.

title: Punct::Valid punctuation character
description: Punct can only be constructed with a language-permitted punctuation character; construction with any other char panics. This also implicitly requires the char to be ASCII so that casting to u8 is lossless.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Punct

**State:** ValidChar

**State invariants:**
- ch is a member of LEGAL_CHARS
- ch fits in u8 (ASCII) because Punct::new casts ch as u8
- new() initializes span to Span::call_site()

**Evidence:** line 21-22: doc comment states 'The `ch` argument must be a valid punctuation character ... otherwise the function will panic.'; line 28-34: LEGAL_CHARS whitelist and panic!("unsupported character ...") enforce the precondition at runtime; line 36: ch is cast to u8, relying on the whitelist to ensure ASCII; line 24-25: doc comment states default span is Span::call_site(); line 35-39: constructor sets span: Span::call_site().0

**Implementation:** Introduce a ValidPunctChar newtype with a checked constructor (e.g., TryFrom<char>) that validates membership in LEGAL_CHARS. Change Punct::new to accept ValidPunctChar instead of char, or provide TryFrom<ValidPunctChar> for Punct. This moves the 'valid punctuation' requirement into the type system so invalid chars cannot be passed to Punct::new at compile time (callers must first construct/obtain a ValidPunctChar).

title: Delimiter::None used in proc_macro output
description: When emitting tokens from a proc_macro, using the invisible delimiter (None) does not reliably preserve grouping or operator precedence; visible delimiters should be used instead.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Delimiter

**State:** None (output context)

**State invariants:**
- None-delimited groups created/recreated in proc_macro output may have their grouping ignored by rustc
- Operator priorities are not preserved for None-delimited groups emitted by a proc_macro
- Invisible delimiters may be lost when round-tripping a token stream through a string
- Only None-delimited groups originating from macro_rules input are sometimes preserved

**Evidence:** line 20: "Invisible delimiters might not survive roundtrip of a token stream through a string."; line 24: "rustc currently can ignore the grouping of tokens delimited by `None` in the output of a proc_macro."; line 25: "Only `None`-delimited groups created by a macro_rules macro in the input of a proc_macro macro are preserved..."; line 27: "Any `None`-delimited groups (re)created by a proc_macro will therefore not preserve operator priorities..."; line 28-29: "The other `Delimiter` variants should be used instead in this context."

**Implementation:** Introduce a NonNoneDelimiter enum { Parenthesis, Brace, Bracket } and require it when constructing groups for proc_macro output (e.g., Group::new_visible(NonNoneDelimiter, tokens)). Provide From<NonNoneDelimiter> for Delimiter and TryFrom<Delimiter> for NonNoneDelimiter to prevent selecting None at compile time.

title: Punct::Construction precondition
description: Punct can only be constructed from a restricted set of ASCII punctuation characters; otherwise construction panics. The constructor also fixes initial span and spacing-derived jointness.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Punct

**State:** Constructed

**State invariants:**
- Input character ch must be one of LEGAL_CHARS
- If ch is not legal, Punct::new panics with "unsupported character ..."
- Stored byte ch is ASCII-compatible (cast via ch as u8)
- Initial span is Span::call_site()
- joint flag reflects spacing == Spacing::Joint

**Evidence:** line 159: doc comment: "The `ch` argument must be a valid punctuation character ... otherwise the function will panic."; line 166: LEGAL_CHARS declared as the whitelist of allowed punctuation; line 170: runtime check of LEGAL_CHARS.contains(&ch); line 171: panic!("unsupported character `{:?}`", ch) names the precondition; line 174: ch stored as u8, which relies on ASCII-only input ensured by the whitelist; line 176: span initialized with Span::call_site().0; line 175: joint set from spacing == Spacing::Joint

**Implementation:** Introduce a validated PunctChar newtype (e.g., struct PunctChar(u8)) with TryFrom<char> that rejects non-LEGAL_CHARS; change Punct::new to accept PunctChar instead of char so invalid inputs are unrepresentable at call sites.

title: Ident::Normal identifier state
description: Normal (non-raw) identifier created by Ident::new; the provided string must be a valid Rust identifier. Printing does not include the r# prefix.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Normal

**State invariants:**
- is_raw == false for normal identifiers
- Input string must be a valid identifier; otherwise construction panics
- Display omits the "r#" prefix in this state

**Evidence:** line 249: doc comment: input must be a valid identifier; otherwise the function will panic; line 271-276: Ident::new sets is_raw: false and constructs from Symbol::new_ident(string, false); line 311-315: Display writes "r#" only when self.0.is_raw, so Normal prints without the prefix

**Implementation:** Model as Ident<State> with zero-sized states Raw and Normal. Ident::new returns Ident<Normal>; Ident::new_raw returns Ident<Raw>. Implement Display separately per state so the r# prefix is encoded in the type rather than a runtime boolean.

title: Ident::Raw identifier state
description: Raw identifier created by Ident::new_raw; string must be a valid identifier but certain path-segment keywords are disallowed and cause a panic. Printing includes the r# prefix.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Ident

**State:** Raw

**State invariants:**
- is_raw == true for raw identifiers
- String must be a valid identifier; some keywords like self/super are not supported and will panic
- Display includes the "r#" prefix in this state

**Evidence:** line 280-283: doc comment: keywords usable in path segments (e.g., self, super) are not supported and will cause a panic; line 284-289: Ident::new_raw sets is_raw: true and constructs from Symbol::new_ident(string, true); line 311-315: Display prepends "r#" when self.0.is_raw

**Implementation:** Use Ident<Raw> as a distinct type from Ident<Normal>. Only Ident<Raw> displays with the r# prefix. Construction-time validation still occurs, but the raw/non-raw distinction is encoded in the type rather than a runtime boolean.

title: Span::Same-file joinability
description: join() only succeeds when both spans originate from the same file.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** SameFile

**State invariants:**
- self and other have the same underlying file identity
- join(self, other) returns Some(..) iff SameFile holds

**Evidence:** line 114: comment explicitly states 'Returns `None` if `self` and `other` are from different files.'; line 116: signature pub fn join(&self, other: Span) -> Option<Span>

**Implementation:** Brand spans by file: struct Span<F>(.., PhantomData<F>); join(&self, other: &Span<F>) -> Span<F>. Provide a fallible downcast fn try_into_file_branded(self) -> Result<Span<F>, _> when a file id is available.

title: Span::Real-source text availability
description: source_text() returns Some only for spans that correspond to real source code.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** RealSource

**State invariants:**
- Span maps to real source (not purely synthetic/remapped)
- source_text().is_some() iff RealSource holds

**Evidence:** line 141: 'It only returns a result if the span corresponds to real source code.'; line 148: signature pub fn source_text(&self) -> Option<String>

**Implementation:** Introduce a RealSourceSpan newtype obtained via try_from(Span) that succeeds only when real source is present; expose source_text() on RealSourceSpan to avoid Option at call sites.

title: Span::Local filesystem path availability
description: local_file() is only available when the span’s source maps to an actual local filesystem path (unaffected by remapping).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** HasLocalFile

**State invariants:**
- Span is associated with a concrete on-disk path
- local_file().is_some() iff HasLocalFile holds

**Evidence:** line 102: doc describes 'The path to the source file ... on the local file system.'; line 108: signature pub fn local_file(&self) -> Option<PathBuf>

**Implementation:** Provide a LocalFileSpan newtype via try_from(Span) that guarantees a local on-disk path; expose local_path(&self) -> &Path on LocalFileSpan.

title: Span::Def-site hygiene context
description: Span representing macro definition-site hygiene; identifiers resolve as at the macro definition site.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** DefSite

**State invariants:**
- Name resolution behaves as if at the macro definition site
- Constructed via Span::def_site()

**Transitions:**
- Any -> DefSite via def_site()

**Evidence:** line 20: 'A span that resolves at the macro definition site.'; line 22: pub fn def_site() -> Span { ... }

**Implementation:** Model hygiene as a type parameter: Span<H> where H ∈ {DefSite, CallSite, MixedSite}; def_site() -> Span<DefSite>. Methods that combine location and resolution (resolved_at/located_at) operate at the type level on H.

title: Span::Call-site hygiene context
description: Span representing macro call-site hygiene; identifiers resolve as if written at the call site and are referable from the call site.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** CallSite

**State invariants:**
- Name resolution behaves as at the macro call location (call-site hygiene)
- Constructed via Span::call_site()

**Transitions:**
- Any -> CallSite via call_site()

**Evidence:** line 26: 'The span of the invocation of the current procedural macro.'; lines 27-29: docs describe call-site hygiene and referability from the call site; line 31: pub fn call_site() -> Span { ... }

**Implementation:** Use Span<CallSite> to represent call-site hygiene; only expose APIs that semantically rely on call-site behavior for this state.

title: Span::Mixed-site hygiene context
description: Span representing macro_rules mixed-site hygiene; some names resolve at definition site and others at call site; location is taken from call-site.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Span

**State:** MixedSite

**State invariants:**
- Resolution: def-site for locals/labels/$crate; call-site for other names
- Location (line/column/file) taken from call site
- Constructed via Span::mixed_site()

**Transitions:**
- Any -> MixedSite via mixed_site()

**Evidence:** lines 35-38: docs describe mixed-site resolution rules and that location is from call-site; line 40: pub fn mixed_site() -> Span { ... }

**Implementation:** Encode hygiene flavor at the type level: Span<MixedSite>. Provide resolved_at/located_at as type-level transformers between hygiene and location components.

title: FilteredTests::Consistent ID allocation state
description: Test and bench entries draw IDs from a single monotonically increasing counter shared via next_id; each add_* assigns the current counter to the new item and then advances it.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Consistent

**State invariants:**
- next_id is the next free ID to assign across both tests and benches
- Each insertion uses TestId(next_id) and increments next_id exactly once
- IDs assigned so far are unique and form a contiguous prefix [0..next_id) if constructed from empty
- After k insertions since creation, next_id has increased by k

**Transitions:**
- Consistent -> Consistent via add_bench() (assigns TestId(next_id), then next_id += 1)
- Consistent -> Consistent via add_test() (assigns TestId(next_id), then next_id += 1)

**Evidence:** line 9: next_id: usize — dedicated counter field tracking allocation state; line 15: self.benches.push((TestId(self.next_id), test)) — benches use the shared counter; line 16: self.next_id += 1 — counter advanced after bench insertion; line 20: self.tests.push((TestId(self.next_id), test)) — tests use the same shared counter; line 21: self.next_id += 1 — counter advanced after test insertion; line 23: fn total_len(&self) -> usize { — total_len derived from both collections; line 24: self.tests.len() + self.benches.len() — suggests next_id should track total inserted items if starting empty

**Implementation:** Introduce a private IdAllocator(newtype around usize) that is the only way to construct TestId. Store IdAllocator inside FilteredTests and make TestId's constructor private to the module. add_test/add_bench obtain IDs via allocator.next(), ensuring unique monotonic assignment at compile time (callers cannot fabricate TestId or skip advancing the counter). Optionally provide a constructor that initializes tests, benches as empty and next_id = 0 to guarantee total_len == next_id.

title: Literal::Finite float input precondition
description: Float constructors accept only finite values; NaN or infinity will panic at runtime.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** FiniteFloatInput

**State invariants:**
- n.is_finite() must be true
- Non-finite inputs cause panic with "Invalid float literal {n}"

**Evidence:** line 62-66: docs state the function will panic if float is infinity or NaN; line 68-70: if !n.is_finite() { panic!("Invalid float literal {n}") } in f32_unsuffixed; line 93-95: same finite check/panic in f32_suffixed; line 113-115: same finite check/panic in f64_unsuffixed; line 138-140: same finite check/panic in f64_suffixed

**Implementation:** Introduce FiniteF32/FiniteF64 newtypes with TryFrom<f32/f64> that reject NaN/Inf; change f32_*/f64_* constructors to take FiniteF32/FiniteF64 so non-finite inputs are a compile-time type error rather than a runtime panic.

title: Literal::Character state
description: character_value() is only valid when the literal kind is Char.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** Char

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::Char
- character_value() returns Ok(char) only for Char literals
- Other kinds return Err(ConversionErrorKind::InvalidLiteralKind)

**Transitions:**
- Any -> Char via character()

**Evidence:** line 156-166: Literal::character(...) constructs a Literal with kind Char; line 301-307: character_value() matches on bridge::LitKind::Char, otherwise returns InvalidLiteralKind

**Implementation:** Model as Literal<Char>; provide character(ch) -> Literal<Char>; expose character_value() only on Literal<Char> so mismatched calls are a compile error.

title: Literal::Byte-character state
description: byte_character_value() should be valid only when the literal kind is Byte; other kinds should be rejected.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** Byte

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == bridge::LitKind::Byte
- byte_character_value() returns Ok(u8) only for Byte literals
- Other kinds return Err(ConversionErrorKind::InvalidLiteralKind)

**Transitions:**
- Any -> Byte via byte_character()

**Evidence:** line 168-178: Literal::byte_character(...) constructs a Literal with kind Byte; line 288-297: byte_character_value() returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind) on kind mismatch (docstring explicitly says 'byte character literal')

**Implementation:** Model as Literal<Byte>; provide byte_character(u8) -> Literal<Byte>; expose byte_character_value() only on Literal<Byte>.

title: Literal::String state
description: str_value() is only valid when the literal kind is Str or StrRaw.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** StrOrStrRaw

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind ∈ {Str, StrRaw(_)}
- str_value() returns Ok(String) for Str/StrRaw
- Other kinds return Err(ConversionErrorKind::InvalidLiteralKind)

**Transitions:**
- Any -> Str via string()

**Evidence:** line 144-154: Literal::string(...) constructs a Literal with kind Str; line 313-337: str_value() handles Str, unescaping as needed; line 338: str_value() returns Ok(...) for StrRaw(_); line 339-340: Otherwise returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind)

**Implementation:** Use Literal<Str> and Literal<StrRaw> types; make str_value() available only for those state types.

title: Literal::C string state
description: cstr_value() is only valid when the literal kind is CStr or CStrRaw.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** CStrOrCStrRaw

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind ∈ {CStr, CStrRaw(_)}
- cstr_value() returns Ok(Vec<u8>) with trailing NUL for CStr/CStrRaw
- Other kinds return Err(ConversionErrorKind::InvalidLiteralKind)

**Transitions:**
- Any -> CStr via c_string()

**Evidence:** line 192-202: Literal::c_string(...) constructs a Literal with kind CStr; line 347-369: cstr_value() processes CStr with escaping and returns Ok or an error; line 370-377: cstr_value() handles CStrRaw(_) and returns Ok with trailing NUL; line 378-379: Otherwise returns Err(InvalidLiteralKind)

**Implementation:** Use Literal<CStr> and Literal<CStrRaw>; expose cstr_value() only on those, ensuring misuse is a compile error.

title: Literal::Byte string state
description: byte_str_value() is only valid when the literal kind is ByteStr (and likely ByteStrRaw).

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** ByteStr

**State invariants:**
- self.0.kind == ByteStr (and possibly ByteStrRaw)
- byte_str_value() returns Ok(Vec<u8>) for ByteStr
- Other kinds return Err(ConversionErrorKind::InvalidLiteralKind)

**Transitions:**
- Any -> ByteStr via byte_string()

**Evidence:** line 180-190: Literal::byte_string(...) constructs a Literal with kind ByteStr; line 385-391: byte_str_value() arm begins with bridge::LitKind::ByteStr handling

**Implementation:** Use Literal<ByteStr> (and Literal<ByteStrRaw> if supported); expose byte_str_value() only on those.

title: Literal::subspan UTF-8 boundary protocol
description: Ranges passed to subspan should align to UTF-8 character boundaries of the source; otherwise downstream printing may panic.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** Literal

**State:** ValidUtf8BoundaryRange

**State invariants:**
- Range start and end are at UTF-8 boundaries within self.span()
- Out-of-bounds ranges cause None; non-boundary ranges risk panics elsewhere per FIXME

**Evidence:** line 219-221: FIXME comment to check that the byte range starts and ends at a UTF-8 boundary, otherwise a panic may occur elsewhere; line 216-218: API returns None only for out-of-bounds, not for non-boundary ranges

**Implementation:** Introduce a CharBoundary type or a checked constructor that produces a Range<usize> guaranteed to be on char boundaries for a given Span; make subspan accept only this validated range.

title: TokenStream::Empty (None-backed) state
description: TokenStream has no underlying bridge handle (self.0 == None). Many operations treat this as an empty stream; some methods (like expand_expr) are invalid and return an error.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == None represents an empty stream
- is_empty() returns true via unwrap_or(true)
- Display prints nothing
- expand_expr() returns Err(ExpandError) when called on this state
- Builders avoid creating a handle for empty inputs

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Present via From<TokenTree>::from() (constructs Some(..))
- Empty -> Present via FromIterator<TokenTree>::from_iter with non-empty input
- Empty -> Present via Extend<TokenTree>::extend with non-empty input
- Empty -> Present via FromIterator<TokenStream>::from_iter when at least one non-empty input exists
- Empty -> Present via Extend<TokenStream>::extend when at least one non-empty input exists

**Evidence:** line 107: pub struct TokenStream(Option<bridge::client::TokenStream>); — Option encodes presence/absence; line 164: TokenStream(None) returned by TokenStream::new(); line 169-171: is_empty() uses self.0.as_ref().map(...).unwrap_or(true) — None => true; line 184-186: expand_expr() errors on None via ok_or(ExpandError)?; line 221-226: Display prints nothing in None branch; line 289-293: ConcatTreesHelper::build returns TokenStream(None) if trees.is_empty(); line 316-320: ConcatStreamsHelper::push skips streams with self.0 == None; line 322-325: ConcatStreamsHelper::build returns TokenStream(self.streams.pop()) — yields None when no inputs

**Implementation:** Introduce TokenStream<S> with zero-sized states Empty and Present. new() -> TokenStream<Empty>. Provide From<TokenTree> -> TokenStream<Present>. Define extend/from_iter to return a new state (Empty or Present). Only implement expand_expr() for TokenStream<Present> so calling it on Empty is a compile-time error.

title: TokenStream::Present (Some-backed) state
description: TokenStream holds a bridge handle (self.0 == Some(..)). Methods that require a backing stream can operate; emptiness of the underlying stream is delegated to the bridge.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** TokenStream

**State:** Present

**State invariants:**
- self.0 == Some(handle)
- Display delegates to BridgeMethods::ts_to_string(handle)
- expand_expr() is callable (precondition satisfied with respect to presence)
- Extending/concatenating keeps the stream backed (Some(..))

**Transitions:**
- Present -> Present via Extend<TokenTree>::extend (concat keeps Some(..))
- Present -> Present via Extend<TokenStream>::extend (concat keeps Some(..))
- Present -> Present via FromIterator<...>::from_iter (concatenation)

**Evidence:** line 263-265: From<TokenTree> for TokenStream produces TokenStream(Some(...)); line 169-171: is_empty() calls BridgeMethods::ts_is_empty on Some(handle); line 221-225: Display writes via BridgeMethods::ts_to_string(ts) for Some; line 184-189: expand_expr() proceeds when self.0 is Some(..); line 301-302: ConcatTreesHelper::append_to sets stream.0 = Some(concat); line 334-339: ConcatStreamsHelper::append_to sets stream.0 = Some(concat) when appending

**Implementation:** Model as TokenStream<Present> when self.0 is Some(..). Provide methods like expand_expr() only on TokenStream<Present>. Concat/extend consume self and return TokenStream<Present>, preserving the backed state.

title: FilteredTests::Empty state
description: Freshly constructed container with no entries; ID counter at zero.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Empty

**State invariants:**
- tests.len() == 0
- benches.len() == 0
- next_id == 0

**Transitions:**
- Empty -> Building via add_test(...)
- Empty -> Building via add_bench(...)

**Evidence:** line 342: let mut filtered = FilteredTests { tests: Vec::new(), benches: Vec::new(), next_id: 0 };

**Implementation:** Introduce FilteredTests<Empty> with only a constructor; transition to FilteredTests<Building> via a builder/init method. Only Building exposes add_test/add_bench.

title: FilteredTests::Building state
description: Tests/benches are being added; IDs are assigned monotonically as items are inserted; vectors are partitioned by role.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Building

**State invariants:**
- next_id == total_len()
- All (TestId, ..) pairs have unique, monotonically increasing TestId in insertion order
- add_bench must only be called for bench variants; add_test only for non-bench variants (enforced by caller logic, not the type system)

**Transitions:**
- Building -> Ready via finishing population (no more mutations) and handing out the collections

**Evidence:** line 286-290: add_bench pushes (TestId(self.next_id), ...) then increments next_id; line 291-295: add_test pushes (TestId(self.next_id), ...) then increments next_id; line 296-298: total_len() derives from tests.len() + benches.len() (implying next_id should track total_len); line 353-360: caller matches bench variants to call add_bench, otherwise add_test (protocol by convention)

**Implementation:** Provide a FilteredTestsBuilder that only exposes add_test(TestDesc, NonBenchFn) and add_bench(TestDesc, BenchFn) where NonBenchFn/BenchFn are newtypes constructed only from matching enum variants. Builder tracks count internally, then finalize(self) -> FilteredTests<Ready>.

title: FilteredTests::Ready/Consumed state
description: After population, totals are reported and the tests collection is moved out for execution; further mutation would break accounting assumptions.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** FilteredTests

**State:** Ready

**State invariants:**
- filtered.total_len() used for reporting is consistent with contents
- tests are moved out exactly once for scheduling
- No further add_* calls should occur after totals are computed

**Evidence:** line 363-370: total_len() is used to report filtered counts and seed; line 374: let mut remaining = filtered.tests; (consumes tests for scheduling, implying a terminalization of building for tests)

**Implementation:** Make add_* methods unavailable on FilteredTests<Ready>; expose read-only accessors. Move-to-queue consumes FilteredTests<Ready> or its tests field via a method to prevent accidental further mutation.

title: Static-only input precondition
description: These entry points require all provided tests to be static; any dynamic test triggers a panic at runtime.

evidence.explanation:
**Entity:** test_main_static + make_owned_test

**State:** StaticOnly

**State invariants:**
- All test functions provided must be StaticTestFn or StaticBenchFn
- Dynamic tests are rejected with a panic
- In spawned secondary abort mode, only static tests are allowed; benches are disallowed

**Evidence:** line 177-182: docstring explicitly states it will panic when fed any dynamic tests; line 241-246: make_owned_test panics with "non-static tests passed to test::test_main_static" on non-static variants; line 221-223: in abort secondary mode, panic!("only static tests are supported") if runnable_test.is_dynamic(); line 226-228: panic!("benchmarks should not be executed into child processes") when encountering Runnable::Bench in child

**Implementation:** Define a StaticOnly<T> wrapper that can only be constructed from StaticTestFn/StaticBenchFn (e.g., via TryFrom with a rejected error for dynamic). Change test_main_static/test_main_static_abort signatures to accept &[&StaticOnly<TestDescAndFn>] so non-static inputs are a compile error.
